<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.7) -->

# `kubectl run`: Image Entrypoint Shapes Argument Handling

The arguments passed after `--` to `kubectl run` (or `kubectl exec`,
`kubectl debug`, plain `docker run`) become `args` to the container.
What runs is `ENTRYPOINT + args`. The image's `ENTRYPOINT` determines
whether you should pass the full command or just its arguments.

## The Rule

Before writing `kubectl run --image=<img> -- <cmd>`, check the image's
entrypoint:

```bash
docker image inspect <img> | jq '.[].Config.Entrypoint'
```

Then pick the argument shape:

| `ENTRYPOINT`                        | Pass after `--`                       |
|-------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Empty (`null` or `[]`)              | Full command, e.g. `mc admin info local` |
| Shell (`["/bin/sh", "-c"]`)         | Full command as a single string       |
| The CLI itself (`["mc"]`)           | Subcommand and flags only, e.g. `admin info local` |
| Wrapper script (`["/entrypoint.sh"]`) | Whatever the wrapper script expects   |

## Concrete failure mode

`quay.io/minio/mc:latest` has `ENTRYPOINT ["mc"]`. The naive command:

```bash
kubectl run mc --rm -it --image=quay.io/minio/mc:latest -- mc admin info local
```

actually runs `mc mc admin info local` inside the container. `mc` does
not recognize a subcommand named `mc`, so the call either no-ops with
an unhelpful error or silently fails depending on version.

Correct form:

```bash
kubectl run mc --rm -it --image=quay.io/minio/mc:latest -- admin info local
```

## Why this matters

- The failure is often silent or produces a misleading "command not
  found" error that masks the real cause (duplicated binary name).
- Tooling images (`mc`, `aws`, `kubectl`, `psql`, `redis-cli`) commonly
  set the CLI as the entrypoint specifically so `docker run <img> ...`
  feels like running the CLI directly — but `kubectl run -- <cmd>` users
  often habitually prefix the binary anyway.
- Copy-pasted shell snippets from non-Kubernetes contexts (Docker docs,
  CI scripts) are the most common source of the duplication.

## When to apply

- Any one-off `kubectl run` against an unfamiliar image.
- Any time `kubectl run` produces no output or an error referencing the
  binary name twice.
- Any time you adapt a `docker run` invocation to `kubectl run`.

## When skipping is acceptable

- Images you maintain and whose entrypoint you already know.
- Distroless or shell-entrypoint base images where the full command is
  always required.
